speaker: interviewer
text: Waisman, tape three.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so I was telling you about when the ghetto was being established and-- in, in Skarzysko. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All I can remember is that my sister and my four brothers and my father, to some extent, worked at the munitions factory. My father and mother were terribly worried. What are they going to do with the baby, me? One day, my father comes home, and he's all excited, he has a solution. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Pack the baby's bag, [NON-ENGLISH] bag. And my mom helped me with a few of my belongings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she told me-- I says, I don't want to go anywhere, where am I going? It's not going to be long. You go with your dad, you're going to be with another family, and they're going to hide you, and you'll be fine. This is going to be over very quickly. You'll come home after it's over. So I just gave her the usual hug and kiss, because it's not going to be for long, eh? And we go. I think we took-- what kind of transportation did we use-- horse and buggy that somebody picked us up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went out into the farmland. And we went into this farmer, I was introduced to him. And my father tells me, this is going to be your home for the next little while. Listen to what they tell you. And you'll be helping him with the cows, with the chickens, and everything else. And look, it's a beautiful life, you know, all this freedom there, and, and lots to eat. You'll be great. And I believed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was left there. And I notice he gave him some zlotys, you know, the polish currency, and some jewelry. And he gave me a hug, shook my hand, and left. And I was there with this-- in this farmhouse. They had-- Do you remember this farmer's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I, I-- it completely escapes me. I, I remember the-- the look of the farm, you know, what it looked like. And of course, I remember I was in charge of, of a herd of cows. And it took me a while, first of all, to get used to them. And we didn't get along at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the cows weren't just-- they didn't-- they weren't friendly enough for my liking. And I think maybe it was that I was lonely. I was lonesome for my mom. I was a mama's boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this farmer married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, he was married. And-- and in fact, there were a couple of kids there. Again, I couldn't warm up to them for some reason. Life on the farm was so foreign to me, so strange, that I just didn't warm up to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they treat you well?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they had-- well, I didn't think so at the time. But when I think back, they were not cruel to me. And you know, I had a nice bed to, to sleep in. I have-- and it was fairly good food. It wasn't the kind of food I was used to. It was goyische food, you know, and so I wasn't used to it. Later on, when I was in the camps hungry, I was thinking of that food. And then suddenly, it was wonderful and I longed for it. And so, after three weeks or a month-- I lost, you know, sort of track of time. I didn't say anything to them, I just left. And it took me some time marching home, because it was a fair-- I walked. It must have taken me hours and hours, I don't know how many hours. But I-- the-- there was already the ghetto there. So I had to smuggle myself into the ghetto. You know, where the guards didn't see, I found a hole in, in the-- in that wall that they had made around the ghetto. And I knocked at the door, and I said, I'm home, Mom. And of course, she was delighted to see me and hugged me and kissed me. The baby is home! My father took one look, and he took his belt and gave me a spanking. It was the only spanking I ever received from my father. It was the first and last.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do? I gave the farmer all this money and, and the jewels to save you? What are we going to do with you? Why did you come home?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was about to hit me again, when my mother stepped in. [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think secretly she was happy to see me. And of course, she was worried that-- what are they going to do with me. What I did that day saved my life. When the ghetto was liquidated, in 1943 I think it was, '42, 1942. Yeah, I didn't keep track of time, you know, one day was the same as the next day. There was a proclamation in Skarzysko that the SS put out. Anybody harboring a Jewish child, bring him into headquarters. You will receive a sack of flour or a sack of sugar. Those were things that were in short supply. This is how much a Jewish child was worth. I know that were a lot of children survived in hiding but not in Skarzysko. To my knowledge, none of them did. They were all denounced for the reward. It's sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were with this farmer, were you the only Jewish child that he was hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, I was the only one. Maybe if there was another one, another Jewish child, I might have sort of established some sort of a relationship. But I didn't have this luxury, so I was by myself. But again, it saved my life. And there were so many other incidents that I did stupid things that saved my life. Because of all my brothers and sister, my sister and I were the most-- the most unlikely ones to survive. My older brother was an officer in the Polish army. He was James Bond to me, when I-- you know, if you want me to describe him. He knew his way about. And he, he was hardened. He was a man. And so were the rest of my brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But us to survive? And that's why they were worried. The baby, what are we going to do with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so what happened afterwards is that after the ghetto was liquidated, the night before, or the morning before at 4 o'clock in the morning, I was woken up. And there was my brother, my older brother. Again, he had some privilege in the camp. He was able to drive a truck in and out. I don't know exactly the job that he had, but it was one of privilege.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother wakes me up, and she says, you're going with Chaim, with my older brother. I said, where am I going? I don't want to go to another farm. No, you're not going to another farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was sort of a hush, you know. I, I could see there was a tension in the-- in the house. The ghetto was going to be liquidated. They didn't tell me that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got my little suitcase, and they put a few of my belongings. I kissed my mom. Again, if I'd known then that was the last time that I was to see her, I don't know. I might not have gone, or I might have been crying, and screaming, and, and banging on the floor. But you know, I was half asleep. And my father gave me a hug. But my mother, particularly, I remember really hugged me and kissed me. And listen to Chaim and you'll be fine. And I'll see you later, this sort of thing. And he covered me up with a, a blanket on the truck. And he says, don't put your head out and drove me out of the ghetto. We went to another farm, a deserted farm. And he put me up on a hay loft. Stay here, don't you budge. I'll come back and get you. For two days I didn't see him. Three days went in, and I was getting hungry, you know, at night. So I would--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was all by myself, worried sick, not knowing what is happening. I would go in the middle of the night when it was pitch dark, I would go down the hayloft and raid gardens for food and sustain myself that way. So I was beginning to learn to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And finally, one day, he shows up with the truck. And he says, OK, we're going into the camp. I says what camp? Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, well, Abram is there. And Dad is there. You'll be with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I go. He smuggles me into the camp. And he made arrangements for me to work in the [NON-ENGLISH] this paint shop. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was introduced to one of the guys there. And he says, OK this is what you do. You lift up this shell. And it was a, a munitions shell, anti-aircraft shell. It was that high, and it was that thick. And I had a little stamp. And I was stamping this on both sides, with the initials F-E-S. Whatever it meant, I-- to this day I don't know. I was really very fast. And I had no problem doing 3,200 a day. I had 12 hours to do it. We started at 7:00 in the morning till 7:00 at night. Any time Gestapo general was-- and some higher up people, important people and the Gestapo would come to inspect the camp, I remember them bringing me-- bringing them to my section. And I was showing him how quickly I did. And they would say, yeah, oh yeah, very, very good, very good. And sometimes they'd give me an extra ration of food. So I was quite pleased with myself. And adult would have--would have found this difficult to do. My only problem was-- and Abram, my brother next to me gave me advice all the time. He must have made a promise to my mother to look after, you know, his little brother. And he was wonderful. He was-- he just-- you know, in the morning, you know, when we-- before going to work, we had to line up to be counted. And he would pinch my cheek to make him-- make me look healthy. And he put cardboard into my shoes, so-- so that I would appear taller. Because the danger was-- there were no kids to speak of. There were-- I think there was two or three. But in my section, I was the only one. And I was doing work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was very secure at work. You know, I was useful. And the Nazis, the Gestapo, looked at me and, good worker. We like this, this guy. So I wasn't a child to them. I was doing something useful to the German machinery.

speaker: interviewee
text: The problem was going to the barracks to sleep, which means we had to walk a distance. And when we were counted, I could be discovered. So I was never in the front, I was always-- you stay inside, so they won't notice you. And life went on that way, and I was pretty secure. And I was quite happy. At first it was kind of tough doing this, because every time I lifted one of these shells-- they weren't extremely heavy but heavy enough that it rubbed the skin off my fingers. And so I'm told they toughened up. I had bandages all over the place because of a-- the skin were rubbed off, and they were bleeding quite heavily. So I had a little problem at first, but then they hardened up, and I was just away to the races.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you worked a 12 hour day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened at the end of a shift?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end of the shift, we would go to the barracks to sleep. And that was another agony. We slept in these, these bunks. Nothing but straw on it. And there were no, no mattresses or anything. We slept [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in our clothes. We didn't change, we didn't-- there wasn't such a thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the lice were just horrendous. And there were bed bugs. When you turned off the lights, the bed bugs would descend on you. Rats were all over the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: The filth and the, the lack of sanitary conditions. You know, we just went to the bathroom, there was  a latrine there. And we washed in cold water. There was sort of a, a round type of like a-- like a-- you know, where cows eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: A trough.

speaker: interviewee
text: A trough. That's the word I was looking for. And then so you washed at that trough in cold water, just to-- you couldn't wash at all. You'd wash your face.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in, in these kind of conditions, you know, the other enemy was, of course, the dirt, and the lice, and the things that constantly ate at you. Typhoid fever was breaking out. And people were dying left and right with, with this typhoid fever. That--it was a terrible enemy.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was an infirmary, nobody in it. There were nurses' white uniforms, the beds were empty. Because the moment you, you went for, for some medical attention, they took you in the back and, and shoot you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we saw the bodies lying there behind. You know, at first we thought they died of natural causes. But then we started seeing it. You could see it. I mean, after-- at first, they were doing this in the side, and the bodies just showed up. But after a while, you could see them being-- a lot of times they would take them out into the woods, shoot them, and then you could see the bodies being piled up and taken away on a daily basis.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in '41, I was getting on to be 11 years old, going on 40. You grow up very fast. You learn to zigzag and maneuver for yourself. But the one things that I can't get out of my mind is that one morning, you know, as we were getting ready to go to work, I see my brother, who was my-- my sort of-- my angel looking after me. You know, I couldn't do without him. Because he was constantly, constantly looking after me. We separated at work. He went to another section, but then we always joined. And he always you know, hovered over me, and mother hen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abram. And he-- he was only you know, a few years older than me. But in terms of-- he was mechanically inclined. You know, I wanted to grow up and be like him, and not be a tailor like my father. I wanted to be an electrical engineer. Because that's what my brother wanted to be. And he could fix anything. And I sort of inherit that from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're going to work, and suddenly I see him. His eyes are glassy, and he's very pale, and he can't stand up. A doctor from the prisoners, one of our own, says oh, he's got typhoid. So my father worked night shift. I didn't see him. We only saw each other on the weekend. We just waved to one another, as-- as we were going to work, and he was going to the barracks to sleep. So I asked this doctor, I says, what are we going to do? He says, well you can hide him. I had no medication to give him. Give him some water. So at night, I looked after him. In the daytime when my father came home to sleep, he looked after him. And when we walked out in between, he was covered with straw. Because sometimes the Nazi-- the Gestapo men would walk in to see if there's anybody left in the barracks, before the other shift would come home. Do you understand what I'm saying? So after a while, the fever broke. And the doctor says, he's lucky, you know, that he's not dead. We were very anxious to get him back to become a human again. And we started some nourishment. And then he-- when he felt strong enough, we thought he's going to go to work. And we wouldn't have to worry of him being discovered. What type of nourishment were you able to get for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The soup made out of potato peels-- there wasn't much, much value in that-- and of course, the bread that we were getting. Sometimes you would find some potatoes in the soup. And a piece of bread that we would get, a piece of bread that was about inch in diameter and a bit of margarine, sometimes. But most of the time, dry bread. And that was-- can you imagine trying to get back to health, let alone sustain yourself. So it was a slow process. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was-- I had another source of getting some food, which I'll tell you in a short while how that was. But what happened-- so that day, the first day, as he was standing there, you could see. Because he was thin, because he had lost a lot of weight. He didn't have any nourishment for some days. I think it was 10 or 11 days. They picked him out. I knew one of the Gestapo men from work. And I had a very good relationship with him, because you know, he looked up to the kind of work I was doing. I ran up to him. And normally he was quite kind. Kind means you know, he didn't beat me up on a daily basis. And he looked the other way, and he didn't mind me getting an extra ration.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he wanted to show to the others that he could be as brutal as the others. When I asked him, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You know, and, and I asked him to-- can I have my brother? I want to take him back. He hit me over the head. And somebody dragged me back into the barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- I don't remember where exactly he was. I was just completely-- I, I was panicky. Because I knew no good is going to come of it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My heart was racing. I couldn't distinguish anything. My vision got blurred. I, I was in a-- in a complete panic. And all I remember is that they took these people. When they had enough people, they loaded them up on a truck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I watched the truck. You know, there was this closure. And they closed the truck. And out it went, into the woods. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the truck disappeared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I started hearing machine gun fire. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I knew. [SOBS] [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] When-- [SOBS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When that-- when the truck came back empty, [SOBBING] I was devastated. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [SOBBING] He was such a wonderful human being. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I figured out that he was 17 at the time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What did he do to deserve this? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] All he ever did is, is help people around him, including myself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What did he do? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had this misfortune to be born Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was unforgivable. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't come to terms with that. I know I lost my other brothers. But I didn't see it. And it-- there isn't the closure that I have with Abram. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 17 years old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He would never get married. He would never have a family. He would never live. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SOBBING] I miss him. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I gotta get a hold of myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told you before that my father, we looked to him for strength. And he was the strength of everyone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After this episode, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he wasn't the same anymore. I watched. He had black hair still at the time. And he had-- and I watched him turn grey. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to him, Dad what's the matter-- what's wrong? And he didn't answer me. And I saw a tear come down his cheek.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very, very mad at him. I was angry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm sorry now that I'm-- that I was angry. I looked to him. I wanted him to say, don't worry, we'll make it. We've lost Abram, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but we'll still come out of it all right. But he didn't say that. And so in my young years, in my naive years, in not knowing how he felt, I was angry at him. And what I didn't know, and I found out many, many years later. I went to a wedding, to Toronto. And I met somebody that were in the same camp. There are quite a few Skarzysko people in Toronto. And they were telling me about Chaim, who tried to organize something there. And they got caught, and they were shot. And my father witnessed it. I didn't know. And he didn't tell me. He already knew that the ghetto was liquidated. And the people from Skarzysko went all to Treblinka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so his beloved Rivka, who he loved probably more than life itself, was gone. One of his sons was gone. Another son that I was aware of, what that I witnessed, was gone. And he probably looked at me-- I'm imagining this-- and probably thought to himself, how long is he going to make it. And so the-- the-- the humanity, the, the faith that he had in human beings, in civilization, in the civilized world, probably disappeared. Probably he gave up. Because I looked for him, when he was going to work and I was coming home to the barracks, to sleep. I looked for him to wave. I didn't see him. I didn't see him. And to this day, I don't know. I don't know what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he die because he gave up? Did he die of a broken heart? Did he die because somebody shot him because he didn't respond quick enough? What happened to my dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I wish I did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The one thing I am sorry is that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm sorry, Dad. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I was angry at him. I'm sorry I was. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] After that, I contracted typhoid fever. And frankly, I didn't even care at that time. Because I knew that I probably wouldn't make it. I don't know who looked after me. Because at that point, I had nobody left with me from my family. But somebody did, somebody must have covered me up with straw. Somebody must have given me some water. Because I made it. And when I went back to work the first day, lo and behold, again I was weak, and I stumbled. And I got picked out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And before I knew it, I was on the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know something? I worried very much before, and I wanted to live. But when I got on that truck, I came to terms with it, and I really didn't care. And I looked forward to it. We're going to go to break now.
